coredata.dc:title: F-doped silicon quantum dots as a novel fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative detection of new coccine and application in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yulu
Zan, Minghui
Cao, Lei
Peng, Jiahui
Wang, Panyong
Pang, Xinpei
Zhang, Yan
Li, Li
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
coredata.dc:description: As a kind of common colorant, new coccine has been applied in many scenes of daily life. In this study, innovative F-doped silicon quantum dots (F-SiQDs) which prepared by a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment were designed for the detection of new coccine. Systematic studies demonstrate that the obtained F-SiQDs exhibited excellent dispersibility and photostability, with the optimal emission at 475 nm and fluorescence quantum yield reached to 25.19%. The fluorescence intensity can be quenched by new coccine due to the principle of IFE, and the corresponding process was decreased rapidly within 1 min. At the same time, the quenching process showed a notable linear relationship within the concentration of new coccine range from 1–100 μmol/L and 100–200 μmol/L with the detection limits of 0.33 and 0.18 μmol/L, respectively. The developed F-SiQDs were successfully adopted to the determination of new coccine in food samples, and also achieved the desired detection results after being coated on the filter-paper. This work highlights the merits of simplicity, fast response and highly selective for new coccine detection, and implements a potential avenue for the utilization of F-SiQDs in the guidance of food safety and food management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine doping
Silicon quantum dots
New coccine
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of NC using silicon quantum dots
Preparation of paper strips of F-SiQDs and detection of NC.
Fluorescence detection of NC by F-SiQDs
Selectivity of the F-SiQDs for NC detection
Quantitative detection of NC in actual samples
Paper-based strips of F-SiQDs and its application

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 8160071152, 21803075, 61805273, and 81902166), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683) and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the High-level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Introduction Plan of Jiangsu Province (JSSCBS20211442).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of F-SiQDs, Inset: the high-resolution TEM image. (B) Size distribution histogram of F-SiQDs. (C) FT-IR survey spectrum of F-SiQDs. (D) The XPS spectrum of F-SiQDs.

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line), emission spectrum (red line) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) of F-SiQDs. Inset: Photographs of F-SiQDs solutions under visible light (left) and UV light (right), respectively. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the F-SiQDs at different excitation wavelengths (320–430 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-SiQDs after addition of different concentrations of new coccine (from top to bottom, 0–200 μmol/L) in PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Inset: Photographs of F-SiQDs solutions under UV light (λ = 365 nm) with or without the addition of NC. (B) Chromaticity color coordinates in the CIE diagram of the F-SiQDs addition of different concentrations of new coccine. (C-D) Linear relationship calibration plot between F0/F and the concentration of new coccine (1 – 100 μmol/L and 100 – 200 μmol/L, respectively). The error bars correspond to the standard deviation of three measurements.

Normalized FL intensity (F/F0 ) of the F-SiQDs toward new coccine (100 μmol/L) and other potential substances (10-fold or equal concentration towards new coccine). The error bars correspond to the standard deviation of three measurements.

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line), emission spectrum (red line) of F-SiQDs, and UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) of new coccine. (B) The observed (black line, Eobsd) and corrected (red line, Ecor) suppressed efficiency of F-SiQDs after addition of NC solution. (C) Fluorescence lifetimes of steady-state of F-SiQDs and F-SiQDs + NC. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of new coccine (blue line), F-SiQDs (black line) and F-SiQDs + NC (red line).
The synthesis process of F-SiQDs and Schematic illustration of obtained F-SiQDs quenched by new coccine.
Analytical results for the determination of NC in real samples. (n = 5).

head.ce:title: F-doped silicon quantum dots as a novel fluorescence nanosensor for quantitative detection of new coccine and application in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a kind of common colorant, new coccine has been applied in many scenes of daily life. In this study, innovative F-doped silicon quantum dots (F-SiQDs) which prepared by a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment were designed for the detection of new coccine. Systematic studies demonstrate that the obtained F-SiQDs exhibited excellent dispersibility and photostability, with the optimal emission at 475 nm and fluorescence quantum yield reached to 25.19%. The fluorescence intensity can be quenched by new coccine due to the principle of IFE, and the corresponding process was decreased rapidly within 1 min. At the same time, the quenching process showed a notable linear relationship within the concentration of new coccine range from 1–100 μmol/L and 100–200 μmol/L with the detection limits of 0.33 and 0.18 μmol/L, respectively. The developed F-SiQDs were successfully adopted to the determination of new coccine in food samples, and also achieved the desired detection results after being coated on the filter-paper. This work highlights the merits of simplicity, fast response and highly selective for new coccine detection, and implements a potential avenue for the utilization of F-SiQDs in the guidance of food safety and food management.

ce:section.ce:para: The invention of synthetic food colorants has brought a better experience for people to enjoy delicious food. Compared with natural colors, synthetic food colors were widely used in sports drinks, desserts, sauces and other food products because of their low price, excellent color uniformity, effective tinting and less fading [1]. Among the various food colors, azo dyes are one of the main components of all colorants used in food coloring [2]. New coccine (NC) is a common synthetic azo dye added to many food products, but the consumption in large quantities of new coccine can lead to potential diseases due to its azo group and aromatic ring structure, and studies have shown that new coccine can lead to adverse health effects such as diarrhea, migraines, neurobehavioral effects and even cancer when it is over-consumption [3]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), the acceptable daily intake of new coccine for humans is 0–4 mg/kg body weight [4]. In the face of the responsibility to control food safety, it is essential to strictly control and regulate NC levels in beverages and foods. To date, several methods have been proposed to detect the content of new coccine, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], immunoassay [6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [7], electrochemistry and polarography [8,9]. However, there are still some defects in the existing methods of analyzing NC, such as time-consuming, expensive equipment, extensive sample preparations and cumbersome operation are needed, that restrict its development and wide application [10]. At the same time, considering that food products are complex matrices, various interferences from other components may occur in the identification and quantitative process of new coccine. Therefore, simple and accurate alternative methods are needed to overcome this obstacle and achieve sensitive and reliable detection results.

Fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely recognized as a promising and efficient sensing material for micro-component determination in environmental and biological fields due to their simple synthesis, excellent optical tunability and easy surface modification capabilities [11–13]. Among the reported photoluminescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots [14] and noble metal nanoclusters [15], have received wide attention for their special advantages in optical stability. However, their applications were limited by high cost or toxicity issues [16]. Zero-dimensional silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have brought considerable attention and are further considered as alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots and conventional organic dyes in the fields of sensing and bioimaging [17]. It is worth noting that silane-based carbon dots does not only have similar photoluminescence properties compared to other fluorescent nanomaterials, but also offer some additional advantages such as low/nontoxicity, strong photostability, appreciable biocompatibility and earth richness [18–20]. Benefiting from these advantages, SiQDs are widely used in bioimaging [21], biosensors [22], biomarkers [23] and also well-used as fluorescence detectors of pH [24], Fe3+ [25], TNT [26], glyphosate [27], D-penicillamine [28] and so on.

Due to the special properties of silicon quantum dots, other elements doped silicon quantum dots played an important role in broadening the development and application of silicon quantum dots. For example, Zhang and his colleagues synthesized the S-doped silicon quantum dots with the emission wavelength of 425 nm which were composed with L-cysteine as the dopant, and these SiQDs can be used as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ by inner filter effect (IFE) and the electron transfer (ET) principle [29]. Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element and also one of the most common elements on the earth [30]. Although it is rarely found in living organisms, it can be used in several biomaterials. The introduction of F can improve the effectiveness of drug treatment, enhance the protein stability, and fluorinated polymers can easily pass through the lipid bilayer structure of cells due to their high affinity for cell membranes [31,32]. Because of the specific properties of F, it has been introduced into carbon dots to expand the application of the nanoparticles, and has been shown to modulate the optical and electronic properties of graphene in F-doped graphene materials [33,34], as well as improve the photostability and biocompatibility of carbon quantum dots [35]. Moreover, these F-doped carbon dots can also be used for cell imaging [32], nucleus staining [36]and even as a photosensitizer [37] and so on. But the application in silicon quantum dots is still very rare. Therefore, the detection of new coccine based on the fluorescence quenching of fluorine-doped silicon quantum dots is innovative and meaningful.

In the present work, fluorine-doped silicon quantum dots (F-SiQDS) to evaluate the concentration of new coccine were prepared by simple hydrothermal treatment using levofloxacin and APTES (3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane) as fluorine source and silicon source, respectively. The resulting F-SiQDs exhibited good sodium chloride tolerability, conspicuous optical bleaching resistance and excellent water solubility which broaden their applications in potential fields. Besides, the emission of as-prepared F-SiQDs could be selectively quenched by new coccine and there was a remarkable linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and a series of gradient concentrations of NC solution. Furthermore, this method achieved good quantitative analysis results in real samples (sparkling drink, lollipop, ice cream) with admirable percentage recoveries of 95.19–106.51%. Subsequently, the paper-based sensor strip was also developed by combining the F-SiQDs with the strip of filter paper, and the response to the new coccine was successfully tested. Based on these, this proposed fluorescent nanosensor creates a new way to determine the amount of new coccine in food samples.

New coccine (NC), 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), levofloxacin, HCl, NaOH, Urea, Sucrose, Glucose and L-Cysteine were obtained from Titan Scientific (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution, NaBr, NaI, NaCl, NaF, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, Sodium metaphosphate, β-cyclodextrin and Sodium benzoate were acquired from Adamas Reagent (Shanghai, China). KCl, CdH8N2O10, CaCl2, C4H6O4Zn, AlCl3, MnCl2, Ba(NO3)2, NaNO2, NaNO3, Sunset yellow, Tartrazine, Basic red 5 and Acid green 3 were all purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). The commercial reagent kit was bought from Bohu Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water (resistivity ≥ 18.2 MΩ⋅cm) was used all through.

The surface observation of the F-SiQDs were reported by JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) with 200 kV acceleration voltage. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer, Thermo Fisher) was used for collecting the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The FT-IR spectrometer (Agilent Cary 660, United States) was selected to record the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) results. Fluorescence lifetime was measured by photoluminescence Spectrometer (FLS 1000, United Kingdom). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were acquired by the F97Pro FL spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology, China). A U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to analyze the UV absorption spectra of this experiment.

F-SiQDs were prepared from APTES and levofloxacin by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.36 g of levofloxacin was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water with ultrasonic treatment, then 120 μL of APTES was added to the levofloxacin solution and stirred for full mixing at room temperature. Afterward, the resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and the reaction temperature was raised at 200 °C for 8 h. After that, the obtained F-SiQDs solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and collected through a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. In addition, an osmosis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 1000 Da) was used to further purify the sample for 48 h. Finally, the purified F-SiQDs were freeze-dried to acquire solid powder and stored at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NC using silicon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained F-SiQDs (0.2 mL, 1.0 mg/mL) were diluted to 3 mL with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) for the determination of new coccine. Then the different amounts of new coccine solution were dispersed into the diluted F-SiQDs and the mixture was shaken completely for 1 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the above mixed solutions was determined by fixing the excitation wavelength at 375 nm. Relative fluorescence indicators were evaluated and used for concentration analysis of new coccine. The detection selectivity of the F-SiQDs probe was measured under the same operating procedures after the addition of various potential interfering compounds in place of new coccine.

Sparkling drink, lollipop and ice cream were purchased from the local supermarket as food samples. The Sparkling drink was processed with ultrasound for 30 min to remove CO2, and then purified through a polystyrene filter (0.22 μm). Taken 1.0 g of crushed lollipop sample and dissolved in 10 mL warm water, with a polystyrene filter (0.22 μm) to remove large particles. For ice cream, 1.0 g of ice cream was melted by added into 10 mL warm water, and the sample solution was also filtered by a 0.22 μm polystyrene filter. Considering the difference of new coccine content in different samples, the amounts of food samples taken were different in the detection process under the condition of ensuring the same concentration of F-SiQDs. 1 mL of sparkling water and lollipop samples were mixed with quantum dot solution while the ice cream was taken in 3 mL for the test. At the same time, appropriate amounts of different concentrations of new coccine were added to verify the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper strips of F-SiQDs and detection of NC.
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose-based filter paper was cut into 2.0 cm × 8.0 cm dimensions and soaked into prepared F-SiQDs solution for 5 min. Then the paper strips were taken out from the aqueous solution and were dried at room temperature. After that, the strips were immersed in different concentrations of new coccine solutions. The fluorescence quenching response of the paper strip was captured subsequently with a camera under a hand-held UV lamp (λ = 365 nm).

These kinds of water-soluble fluorine-doped silicon quantum dots were designed through a brief and convenient hydrothermal process, as shown in Scheme 1 , by using APTES and levofloxacin as the silicon and fluorine sources. The obtained TEM images of the F-SiQDs (Fig. 1 A) show that the quantum dots were spherical and well dispersed, with sizes ranging from 1.2 − 3.4 nm and an average diameter of 2.25 nm. The high-resolution TEM images in the inset show a clear lattice structure with a spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating a high degree of crystallinity. The diameter of quantum dots in water was measured with 100 particles from the TEM image (Fig. 1B). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of F-SiQDs (Fig. S1A) showed a typical diffraction peak at about 2θ = 22°, which was consistent with the (101) planes of silicon oxide [38]. The elemental components analysis with EDX (Fig. S1B) showed the presence of C, N, O, Si and F elements, equally proving the successful synthesis of the F-SiQDs. Then the functional groups present on the surface of F-SiQD particles were probed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1C). The peak at 3448.0 cm−1 corresponded to N–H stretching vibration which indicated the presence of amino groups on the surface of F-SiQDs [39], which conformed to the phenomenon of positive potential (Fig. S1C). The absorption band at 2935.6 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of the C–H bond and the peak at 1618.0 cm−1 was belonged to the C = O stretching vibration peak in the carbonyl group [40,41]. The absorption peak of the stretching vibrations of the C = C band was observed at 1560.4 cm−1 [42]. Furthermore, the spectrum presents a significant absorption peak of semi-ionic C-F bond which was at 1265.1 cm−1 [43]. The absorption peak at 1052.0 and 977.7 cm−1 were attributed to Si-O-Si and Si-O–H [44], respectively.

Moreover, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to further confirm the surface nanostructure and elemental analysis of F-SiQDs. Six distinct peaks at 101.6, 152.6, 285.7, 399.6 531.6 and 685.6 eV were illustrated in the all-scan of XPS results (Fig. 1D) and were corresponded to Si2p, Si2s, C1s, N1s, O1s and F1s [43,45]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s showed 5 sorts of carbon bonds which were associated with 288.5 (C–F), 286.9 eV (C–O), 286.0 eV (C–N), 284.8 eV (C–C/CC) and 284.0 eV (C–Si) (Fig. S2A) [46,47]. Three fitted peaks at 401.0, 400.0 and 399.2 eV were obtained on the N1s XPS results (Fig. S2B) representing N–(C)3, C-N–C and N–Si [48]. Three dominant peaks were noted on the O1s XPS spectrum. Si-O corresponded to the peak at 533.2 eV and CO and C–O–C were attributed to the other two peaks at 532.0 and 530.2 eV (Fig. S2C), respectively [49]. In Si 2p spectrum (Fig. S2D), the peak at 101.5 eV presaged the presence of Si–C, the other two peaks at 102.0 and 102.8 eV were assigned to Si-N and Si-O, respectively [50]. XPS spectrum of F 1 s (Fig. S2F) illustrated two peaks centered at 686.6 and 687.1 eV corresponding to the covalent C-F bonds and semi-ionic C-F bonds, respectively [47]. Consistent with the FT-IR results, the surface constitution of the Si QDs analyzed by XPS further confirmed the successful introduction of F into silicon quantum dots, while the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups indicates the good water solubility of F-SiQDs.

The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum were used to analyze the optical characters of as-prepared F-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the obtained F-SiQDs exhibit three characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks at 224, 250 and 284 nm, which were ascribed as π → π* transitions of CC bond [43]. The inset of Fig. 2A displayed that the F-SiQDs solution was nearly colorless with sunlight and appeared cyan fluorescence with UV light (365 nm). Through the emission wavelengths of the F-SiQDs (Fig. 2B) at particular excitation spectra (320 – 430 nm) demonstrated the optimal excitation and maximum emission peaks were at 375 and 475 nm, respectively. And the optimal excitation (λex = 375 nm) was selected to complete the follow-up experiments. Meanwhile, it can be seen that the F-SiQDs performed a weak excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, which was assigned to the oxygen induced defects and different surface states disturbing bandgap transition of the F-SiQDs [51]. After calculated by using quinine sulfate as a standard solution, the quantum yield of the F-SiQDs was up to 25.19%, and the procedure for calculating quantum yield is in the supplementary material.

The stability of prepared F-SiQDs was further verified by recording fluorescence intensity under different conditions. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of F-SiQDs with various concentrations of NaCl solution was explored (Fig. S3A), and the fluorescence emission of F-SiQDs still maintained a high fluorescence intensity even when NaCl medium increased to 2 mol/L, which showing the outstanding salt resistance in Na+ and Cl−. As displayed in Fig. S3B, when the temperature rises from 10 °C to 70 °C, although the fluorescence intensity would be affected by high temperature, the fluorescence intensity was relatively stable at room temperature, which does not affect the application under normal conditions. In addition, the fluorescence intensity presented a trend of gradual increase as the pH increased from 2.0 to 12.0 and showed a relatively stable state between pH = 5.0 and 9.0 (Fig. S3C). The variation of fluorescence intensity may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation as a result of the functional groups on the surface of the F-SiQDs [52]. Considering the applicability and stability of the detection system, PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was used to dilute the F-SiQDs solution in subsequent experiments to obtain reliable values. Furthermore, the photostability of F-SiQDs was evaluated by continuous sunlight exposure for 8 h (Fig. S3D), and the almost unchanged fluorescence intensity demonstrated a robust photostability of the F-SiQDs. All these results suggested the prospective applications of the synthesized F-SiQDs in several fields, especially in sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of NC by F-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before exploring the contact between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of new coccine solution, the detection conditions were firstly tested and optimized. The contact between the emission intensity of F-SiQDs and reaction time after the addition of NC was shown in Fig. S4A. The quenching response was completed within 1 min and maintained a stable state in the following 90 min. Therefore, 1 min was selected as the reaction time which further proved the fast response capability of the F-SiQDs. The fluorescence changes of this system under different pH were similar in the presence or absence of NC, and it kept stable at pH = 4.0–9.0 (Fig. S4B). Also for the reliability of the data, PBS buffer solution was selected to provide a stable pH environment as mentioned above.

Under the above conditions, the prepared solutions of a different concentration gradients of NC were added into the quantum dots system, the results were shown in Fig. 3 A, the emission intensity of F-SiQDs was declined distinctly, and the inset also confirmed that the fluorescence of the mixed solution became colorless under the same excitation light after the addition of NC. Besides, it can be seen that the fluorescence spectra changed consistent with the CIE chromaticity diagram results (Fig. 3B). More significantly, the analysis of the data processing revealed two remarkable linear relationships between the value of F/F0 and the concentration of NC, which ranged from 1–100 and 100–200 μmol/L (Fig. 3C-D). F indicates the fluorescence intensity of the experimental system and F0 refers to the F-SiQDs’ fluorescence in the blank. The matched linear regression equations were F0/F = 0.02529c + 0.92565 (R2 = 0.992) and F0/F = 0.04672c – 1.23911 (R2 = 0.994), with calculated limit of detection at 0.33 μmol/L and 0.18 μmol/L, respectively. Compared with the previous work of detection with new coccine (Table S1), this based fluorescence method presented a pleasant detection effect than most reported methods, which established the foundation for the subsequent determination of NC concentration in various situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the F-SiQDs for NC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Specificity is an important indicator of the practical value of quantum dots, and excellent selectivity is more important in the detection of food containing a variety of complex ingredients. Firstly, common metal ions (Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Na+, Zn2+,) anions (F-, Br-, NO2–, SO3 2-, NO3 –, SO4 2-, I-, Cl-), small molecular interferers (Cys, Sucrose, Urea, Glucose) and preservatives (β-cyclodextrin, Sodium benzoate and Sodium metaphosphate) that may be used in common food were added to the detection system. It was obvious that no efficient changes in the fluorescence of quantum dots towards these interferers even at 10-fold concentration than that of NC (Fig. 4 ). In addition, considering other pigments that might be used in many scenes, the equal concentrations of colorants (Sunset yellow, Tartrazine) and dyes (Basic red 5 and Acid green 3) were selected as interferers, which also did not cause much interference. The results showed that the excellent selectivity of the quantum dots can be capable of the detection of NC in actual samples.

The fluorescence quenching process of quantum dots may be affected by one or more quenching mechanisms. Firstly, the UV–vis absorption of new coccine and the emission spectrum of the F-SiQDs were analyzed carefully. It can be seen that the absorption peak of new coccine had a notable spectral overlap with the emission peak of F-SiQDs (Fig. 5 A), which corresponded to the features of IFE (inner filter effect) and FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) quenching processes [4]. To better confirm the existence of IFE, after measuring the absorption values of F-SiQDs and NC solutions at the excitation (375 nm) and emission (475 nm) wavelength, the IFE mechanism was verified by Equation S2 and the calculated results were listed in Table S2. As shown in Fig. 5B, with the increase of NC concentration, the effect of IFE on the quenching process was estimated based on the observed and corrected fluorescence inhibition efficiency, which further proved the presence of the IFE mechanism [53–55]. Then the dynamic fluorescence lifetimes of detection systems with or without the addition of NC were compared. Since IFE does not affect changes in donor fluorescence lifetime, whereas FRET is characterized by a shortening of the fluorophore lifetime associated with the quenching process [56]. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence lifetimes of the system before and after adding NC were calculated by using the dual-exponential decay function, and the fluorescence lifetimes of the two groups were calculated to be 10.14 ns and 9.89 ns, respectively. The almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes not only excluded the FRET process, but also proved that no dynamic quenching was involved [57]. The variation of the UV–vis absorption spectrum is a commonly used method to evaluate static quenching. As displayed in Fig. 5D, the UV absorption peak of the mixed system has a strict corresponding relationship with the absorption peak of the F-SiQDs and NC, and the absence of a new peak also excluded the existence of static quenching [2]. According to the above analysis, it can be seen that the emission quenching of F-SiQDs with NC was dominated by the IFE principle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of NC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three representative common foodstuffs, Sparkling drink, lollipop and ice cream were selected as detected objects, and an appropriate amount of NC with the known concentration of NC was added to the system for recovery analysis. After the samples were simply processed by the method described above, the fluorescence data were collected and the concentration of NC in the detected mixture was calculated according to the regression equation. The results were shown in Table 1 . The contents of NC in treated samples of sparkling drink, lollipop and ice cream were 31.27 μmol/L, 35.49 μmol/L and 40.36 μmol/L, respectively. The recovery rate after adding NC with known concentration was between 95.19% and 106.51%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.28%–0.58%. This series of results were consistent with the reported studies on the quantitative detection of new coccine in food, further confirming the presence of NC in these samples [4,8]. Additionally, to verify the accuracy of this method, a commercial reagent kit was used for further validation. After the samples were treated with the same method as described in the paper, the content of NC in the solution was measured with the reagent kit and the results were listed in Table S3. It can be seen that there was no significant difference between the two analytical solutions (P value > 0.05) [58]. The results above suggested that the measurement performance of the fluorescence sensor was satisfactory, which further demonstrated its practicality and reliability and it is suitable for the determination of NC in food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based strips of F-SiQDs and its application
ce:section.ce:para: The test strips were regarded as a potential detection tool because of their low cost and biodegradable advantages. After operating according to the experimental part of the paper-based strips of F-SiQDs (Fig. S5), it was obvious that under different concentrations of new coccine, the brightness of the strips weakened with the increase of new coccine, which was consistent with the previous experimental results. This part of the work demonstrated that the onsite detection of new coccine can be accomplished by using paper strips under simple conditions without relevant complex instruments. Meanwhile, the color change of the test strip established the basis for future point-of-care colorimetric sensing methods [59], which will greatly increase the convenience of the work.

In summary, a new specific F-doped silicon quantum dots were productively developed by using levofloxacin as a fluorine source with one-pot hydrothermal treatment in this research. The as-synthesized F-SiQDs showed outstanding water solubility, conspicuous photostability and chemical stability with high fluorescence quantum yield (25.19%). Meanwhile, as a common colorant used in foods and drinks, new coccine can sensitively quench the fluorescence of F-SiQDs through the rule of IFE, and the factual operation of F-SiQDs for the monitoring of new coccine was effectively determined in real samples with gratifying recoveries. Significantly, the portable fluorescent filter paper was prepared for monitoring NC based on the stability of the detection equipment, which increased the convenience and practicality of this project. Based on the wide application of NC in food and its effects on human health, this study has great utility value and potential prospects in food safety and quality monitoring. In addition, this work also provides a reference for the doping of other elements with SiQDs and broadens the application of fluorescent quantum dot sensors.

Yulu Liu: Project administration, Writing – original draft. Minghui Zan: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lei Cao: Validation, Data curation. Jiahui Peng: Formal analysis. Panyong Wang: Software, Data curation. Xinpei Pang: Methodology, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Formal analysis. Li Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Qian Mei: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Wen-Fei Dong: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 8160071152, 21803075, 61805273, and 81902166), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683) and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145), the High-level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Introduction Plan of Jiangsu Province (JSSCBS20211442).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Z. Fan, Y. Zhang, N. Li, S.G. Liu, T. Liu, N.B. Li, H.Q. Luo, A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 240 (2017) 949-955.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-responsive fluorescent sensor for Hg(2+) and thiols based on N-doped silicon quantum dots and its application in cell imaging
J. Mater. Chem. B

Chem. Commun. (Camb.)

Anal. Chim. Acta
